evals.prompt: Set up RSpec for our Rails 7 app with FactoryBot, WebMock, and request specs.
evals.expected_output: Should check qa-context.md or ask. Should walk through adding `rspec-rails`, `factory_bot_rails`, `webmock`, `vcr` to the Gemfile test group; running `rails g rspec:install` to generate `.rspec`, `spec/spec_helper.rb`, `spec/rails_helper.rb`. Should suggest `spec/support/` for shared modules (WebMock setup, FactoryBot include) and require_all in rails_helper. Should show `WebMock.disable_net_connect!(allow_localhost: true)` and `RSpec.configure { |c| c.order = :random; c.filter_run_when_matching :focus }`. Should include a sample request spec and a sample factory. Should use synthetic data.
assertions: Reads qa-context.md or asks for it
Names the right gems (rspec-rails, factory_bot_rails, webmock, vcr)
Uses spec/support pattern for shared modules

evals.prompt: Our RSpec suite has many `let(:user) { create(:user) }` but tests are slow. We don't always need the user to be persisted.
evals.expected_output: Should explain `build_stubbed`: returns a model that looks saved (has id, persisted?) without hitting the DB. Use it whenever the test only reads attributes or method behavior on the model — no associations followed, no save. Show: `let(:user) { build_stubbed(:user) }`. Should mention also that `build` (without stubbed) is faster than `create` when the object is just being passed around in-memory. Should warn that build_stubbed has limits — code that calls .save or follows associations may break.
assertions: Warns about limits (no save, association lookup)
Recommends using only where DB isn't actually needed

evals.prompt: how do i mock an external api call in a spec
evals.expected_output: Should treat the casual phrasing as a valid trigger. Should show WebMock pattern: `stub_request(:post, 'https://billing.example.com/charge').with(body: hash_including(amount: 1999)).to_return(status: 201, body: { id: 'ch_synthetic' }.to_json, headers: { 'Content-Type' => 'application/json' })`. Should mention `WebMock.disable_net_connect!(allow_localhost: true)` ensures unintended calls fail loudly. Should mention VCR as the record-and-replay alternative for third-party APIs you can't easily hand-stub. Should use synthetic data.
assertions: Mentions VCR as the record-replay alternative

evals.prompt: RSpec config has `config.order = :random` and our suite started failing intermittently. Should we disable random order?
evals.expected_output: Should clearly say no — random order is doing its job by surfacing order-dependent specs. Recommend: reproduce with the failing seed (`rspec --seed XXXX`), bisect to find the spec that pollutes state, then fix the root cause (often: shared mutable state, missing teardown, FactoryBot sequence pollution, time / locale state leaking). Should suggest `database_cleaner-active_record` or transactional fixtures for DB cleanup between specs. Should NOT recommend disabling random order. Cross-reference flaky-test-management.
assertions: Frames the failures as RSpec exposing real bugs

evals.prompt: We have lots of controller specs. Should we migrate to request specs?
evals.expected_output: Should recommend yes: controller specs are deprecated and request specs exercise the full Rails stack (router, middleware, full request/response cycle), which catches more real bugs. Migration steps: rewrite `describe MyController, type: :controller` blocks as `describe 'POST /resource', type: :request`, replace `get :show, params: {...}` with `get '/resource/:id', params: {...}`, replace `assigns(:user)` (controller-spec-only) with assertions on response body or DB state. Should warn that some controller-only matchers (like `render_template`) don't have direct request-spec equivalents — assertions should focus on outcomes (status, body, side effects). Cross-reference test-strategy.
assertions: Strongly recommends migrating to request specs
Reframes assertions on outcomes vs internals

evals.prompt: Our 5000-spec suite takes 25 minutes. How do we parallelize?
evals.expected_output: Should recommend `parallel_tests` gem: runs RSpec across multiple processes, splitting by file. Needs per-worker DB isolation: `rake parallel:create parallel:migrate` creates myapp_test1 / myapp_test2 / etc. For cross-machine sharding in CI, configure `parallel_tests` to honor `TEST_ENV_NUMBER`. Should mention `rspec-queue` and `flatware` as alternatives. Should also recommend profiling slow specs (`config.profile_examples = 10`) — sometimes the bottleneck is a few specs you can speed up rather than parallelizing harder. Cross-reference ci-test-orchestration.
